title: 2025/09/17 - EW
displayTitle: 2025-09-17 - Education and Workforce Committee - Education and Training (Early Childhood Education Reform) Amendment Bill
topic: Education and Training (Early Childhood Education Reform) Amendment Bill
description: Wednesday 17 September 2025 | Education and Workforce Committee Education and Training (Early Childhood Education Reform) Amendment Bill Submitters Hon David Seymour, Associate Minister of Education David Haynes Margaret Stuart
descriptionSummary: Education and Training (Early Childhood Education Reform) Amendment Bill. Hon David Seymour, Associate Minister of Education. David Haynes

chunks.text: Um, I won't go into it in any great detail, but, um, well can, if you like. But, uh, in, in short, the early childhood sector, um, has long complaints of the regulatory burden that they face. Um, an enormous amount of anxiety amongst the operators.

Um, a lot of frustration with the volume of compliance and a thought that maybe the underlying objectives of child safety and and educational performance could be better, uh, achieved, uh, with a simplified regime. So the Ministry for Regulation, uh, carried out one of its sector reviews in which it listened to literally thousands of people in the sector, operators, parents, teachers, and so on.

Um, and it came up with 15 recommendations, which the government accepted. Um, this legislation is one part of that process. Uh, part of it is changing the licensing criteria. There's been quite an extensive, uh, consultation carried out by the ministry, uh, on that.

That's, that's not in scope of this bill, except for some, uh, rules around how future licensing criteria might be set. Uh, it also, uh, will change who the regulator is, uh, however that will be performed as an education reform bill, as part of the government's wider restructuring of the education sector.

And that's due, uh, I understand to be introduced by Minister Stanford, uh, later this year. Uh, she's currently working on that, but it will also implement some of the recommendations of this, uh, uh, early childhood sector review. And then there is, uh, this part that we come to today, uh, which is putting in place, uh, some statutory rules around who is ultimately responsible for regulation of ECE and what their procedures are, uh, for regulating.

And the purpose of that, um, I think is probably best brought about by an experience that an early childhood center had. Uh, earlier this year, a very well regarded, much loved by its rural community, uh, went to the brink of shutdown, uh, would've probably been shut down without some gentle ministerial intervention.

Um, and to this day, nobody is quite clear why. Um, I think the reason why is that, uh, there was a regulator in one office of the Ministry of Education, and they, they tend to vary in their reputation with the ece, uh, who had got the PIP with this, uh, ECE and, and really brought to bear a lot of power on it.

Now, the purpose of this legislation is to say there's a clear purpose for, for regulating, um, and that, uh, people who feel that they've got an unfair deal, either as a parent, um, or as, uh, a, um, a regulated party can tap on the sign and say, hang on a minute, parliament has actually said what the purpose of regulating is.

Um, and there is a director who statutorily has the power, uh, who's accountable for ensuring that that's upheld. So really, this is about clarifying everybody's rights and responsibilities and, and purposes in relation to, uh, regulating ECE. And along with the changes in licensing criteria, which is outta scope today, the change of the, uh, education Review Office being the, the regulator separate from the policy maker, which I think is just generally good practice.

Um, these will, will, will put in place the statutory requirements. Um, so that's, that's the gist of it. Um, happy to, to go through it in more, more detail, but, um, that's, that's what we're trying to do here. So I thank you for considering it. Thanks very much. Minister. But having some proportional regulating, uh, I think is really, really important.

'cause that, that creates real anxiety and fear in the sector. And really, we want the regulator to be collaborative, still firm on the stuff that really matters, but also more prepared to act as a guide if there's room for improvement without getting all adversarial. Now, uh, you say, what does that, what does that mean? Uh, and this is the whole point. It's, it's far better to have a regulatory system that brings people inside the tent, allows 'em to operate, focuses proportionately on the stuff that really matters, um, rather than, uh, something that is actually scaring people outta the industry and leaving parents with less choice. 'cause that's, you know, a, a bad thing. Mm-hmm.

Um, and it's also worth just thinking about the choices that parents and children have, um, if you end up scaring them out of the business. Uh, and often the child is actually in a better and safer environment in an ECE where they're learning more than they might be in their own home.

Thank you for hearing my oral submission. I believe that my submission is clear, so in the limited time available, I shall discuss a few salient points.

Firstly, when the associate minister introduced this bill to the house, he stated that the new director of regulation will be responsible for licensing, monitoring, and enforcing compliance. He also stated that the government is shifting licensing certification and enforcement to the Education Review office, yet the bill makes it very clear that the director of regulation will be employed by the Ministry of Education and is accountable to the Secretary for Education.

In my submission, I have assumed that the associate minister's speech to the house was in error and that the director of regulation is as stated in the bill part of the ministry. If the Associate minister's words were correct, then much of the bill will have to be rewritten. Also, when the associate minister introduced this bill to the house, he stated that $3.1 billion of taxpayers money goes into ECE and that parents stump up a comparable amount.

Unfortunately, the associate minister has been unable to produce any evidence to support his statement that parents pay a comparable amount. The best that his officials have been able to provide is that his briefing papers stated that parents provide a smaller but still significant amount of co-funding, smaller, but still significant is not the same as comparable like the associate minister.

I do not know what the total parent contribution is. $100 million or $300 million could be considered significant amounts, but at three to 10% of the government's contribution, these would clearly not be comparable. My reason for raising this is not that this bill is affected by the actual amount that parents pay, but to highlight some of the erroneous statements being used to justify to the House of Representatives that this bill is actually necessary.

That said, there are some good things in this bill. The new objectives are commendable and is gratifying to see that the first two objectives focus on good outcomes for Tamaki. I do, however, take issue with the rewording of the purpose of part two of the act, supporting the choice of parents and caregivers to participate in the labor market has no place in this act.

It is a laudable objective, but the Education and Training Act is no place for it. If it were, then the same purpose would be included in parts one and three of the act. You will be aware that parental participation in the labor market currently appears nowhere in the act.

Included in part two would be anomalous and would be a hallmark of sloppy legislation. Similarly, creating a director of regulation just for ECE would be anomalous. It is gratifying to see ECE become the subject of efforts to improve its regulation. It has for a long time been subject to successful lobbying from the for-profit sector, seeking the easing of compliance to increase profits rather than to benefit children.

But creating a director just for ECE and for nowhere else seems odd. In my submission. I have mentioned additional functions for which the director should be made statutorily independent, and I have suggested some tightening of the director's ability to delegate their functions, but there is no time to dwell on those.
